prompt: Hood-mounted camera through the windshield: [SUBJECT A] drives, [SUBJECT B] in the passenger seat, mid-argument at night — "You don't get to decide that for both of us." Streetlights wash through the cabin in rhythmic pulses, lighting and hiding their faces in turn. The driver's eyes flick between the road and the fight. 35mm, both held in frame with nowhere to go. Tires, blinker, silence weaponized.

title: Two Languages, One Table
prompt: Three people at a table in [LOCATION: a warm restaurant]: a negotiation conducted through a translator. The camera pans slowly between speakers, lagging deliberately — holding on the listener who doesn't understand yet, catching the flicker when the translation lands soft on a hard sentence. [SUBJECT] watches the original speaker's eyes, not the translator's mouth — reading the intent before the words arrive, a flicker of understanding crossing their face one beat early. 50mm, warm light, the scene living in the delays between languages.

title: Walk-and-Talk Oner
prompt: Steadicam glides backward through a busy newsroom ahead of [SUBJECT] and a colleague mid-rapid-fire argument — "We run it tonight or we don't run it" — weaving around desks, a third character joining mid-stride to hand off a page and peel away, phones ringing through the dialogue, the two leads arguing eyes-forward and saving direct looks for only the lines that matter. One unbroken take, the walk's pace driven by the argument's tempo. 28mm, fluorescent daylight mix, rapid-fire walk-and-talk velocity.

title: First Date Fumble
prompt: Two-camera-feel coverage of a first date: matched slow pushes on [SUBJECT A] and [SUBJECT B] across a small candle, both talking slightly too fast, a reached-for water glass nearly knocked, the laugh that finally lands — eye contact held a half-second past safe — resetting both their shoulders. Background diners softened to fairy lights. The pushes arrive at close-ups exactly as the conversation finds its footing.

title: Doorway Breakup
prompt: The breakup staged on a threshold: [SUBJECT A] inside the apartment, [SUBJECT B] in the hallway, the doorframe splitting the frame and the lighting — warm interior against cold corridor. "Just say it here, then." — said to the floor, eyes lifting only for the answer. The door's edge under one hand the whole scene, the architecture asking the question before anyone answers it. 40mm static. The close is a door, slow, no slam.

title: Garage Talk, Father and Son
prompt: A locked wide in a garage at night: father under the hood, son handing tools, the real conversation conducted entirely sideways — "She asked about you" answered with "Pass the three-eighths." Long silences filled by ratchet clicks, the son stealing glances at his father's profile that are never returned until the very last line. Neither face fully visible; the worklamp lights the engine, not them. The almost-said thing arriving in the last line, addressed to the carburetor.

title: The Fifty-Minute Hour
prompt: Therapy scene in patient statics: [SUBJECT] on the couch in a clean single, the therapist's questions arriving from off-frame more than on — "And what did you do with that?" — long permitted silences — [SUBJECT]'s eyes traveling to the window when the answer gets close, returning when it's safe — a tissue box in the frame's corner doing silent work. One reframe only, when the session turns: slightly closer, earned. Soft daylight, a clock neither shot includes but both feel.

title: It's Not Working Out
prompt: The firing scene shot for the fired: [SUBJECT] in a clean single across the desk while the rehearsed phrases arrive from off-screen — "restructuring," "effective today," "we'll need your badge" — the camera holding their face through the gap between hearing it and absorbing it: a slow blink, eyes dropping to the desk's edge, the careful reassembly of expression before they trust their voice. A folder slid into frame. The single security-escort question asked politely. Fluorescent honesty, no score.

title: The Unplanned Proposal
prompt: Anti-spectacle proposal: mid-argument about groceries in a kitchen, [SUBJECT] stops, laughs at something invisible, and asks — no kneeling, no ring yet, just the question arriving years early and exactly on time. The handheld camera settles as the room does. Their eyes search each other — disbelief, then the smile arriving before permission. The answer is a question back: "Are you serious?" twice, differently. Dish towel still in hand throughout. Evening window light.

title: Wedding Toast Landmine
prompt: A wedding toast that knows too much: the speaker's glass raised, the story starting funny and drifting one detail past safe — the camera pushing slowly on [SUBJECT] at the head table as cutaways collect the room's recalibrating faces, a fork set down, a bridesmaid's glance. The save attempted, half-landing. Warm string lights, applause arriving a beat late and a touch loud.

title: Wide Two-Shot: Room Enough to Hide In
prompt: Full-room wide two-shot for an argument: [SUBJECT A] at the window, [SUBJECT B] by the door, an entire [LOCATION: living room] of distance between them doing the emotional staging. Neither closes the gap during the hardest lines — eyes finding each other across the room and breaking away to furniture, to floor. One step forward late in the scene reads like an earthquake. The wide never cuts; proximity is the plot.

title: Medium Two-Shot: Park Bench
prompt: Classic bench coverage in a single medium two-shot: [SUBJECT A] and [SUBJECT B] side by side facing out, the conversation conducted mostly to the middle distance — easier to say hard things to a pond than a face. Eye contact happens twice: once by accident, broken instantly; once on purpose at the turn, held. A pigeon negotiation in the foreground gives their hands somewhere to look. Shoulders end the scene one inch closer.

title: Shot/Reverse: Matching Close-Ups
prompt: The intimacy workhorse: matching close-up coverage, 65mm, eyelines tight to the lens on both sides so the audience sits inside the conversation. [SUBJECT A]'s eyes searching [SUBJECT B]'s face line by line; [SUBJECT B]'s reverse catching the exact moment a defense fails — a blink rate change, the mouth's small surrender before the words agree. Cut on the looks, not the lines. Backgrounds melted to nothing; only faces remain.

title: 50/50 Profile: Neither Yields
prompt: Confrontation in a 50/50 profile two-shot: [SUBJECT A] and [SUBJECT B] face to face in [LOCATION: a bare room], matching profile, equal halves of the frame, neither given the camera's allegiance. Eyes locked the entire scene — the dialogue almost secondary to the staring contest under it. Jaw muscles, a slow blink deployed like punctuation, the first person to look away losing something the script never names. Symmetry as referee.

title: Dirty Singles: The Negotiation
prompt: Negotiation coverage in dirty singles — each close-up keeping the rival's blurred shoulder in frame so neither face is ever alone. [SUBJECT A] reads a number off a page and watches it land: [SUBJECT B]'s eyes hold steady but the pen stops moving — the tell. Counter-offer delivered with a small accommodating smile that the eyes decline to join. The pauses negotiated as hard as the figures. [LOCATION: Glass conference walls, city behind], everything reflective.

title: Diner Booth Coverage
prompt: Booth scene shot in cross coverage: window light raking [SUBJECT A], neon edging [SUBJECT B], coffee refills marking the scene's acts. The conversation circles its real subject for a page — eyes meeting over mugs and retreating to the table's sugar geography — until [SUBJECT A] says the actual thing while looking out the window, and [SUBJECT B]'s reflection in the same glass closes its eyes. The waitress's approach resets them both to performance.

title: Back Seat Confession
prompt: Two passengers in a cab's back seat, locked frame, the city strobing past behind them in passing sodium pulses. [SUBJECT A] tells it quietly, eyes on the driver's headrest because the truth is easier said to upholstery; [SUBJECT B] turns fully to face them — the only big movement in the scene — and waits, eyes open and patient, for the rest. Streetlight crosses both faces at the exact line that changes things. The driver's eyes flick once in the mirror.

title: Walking Apology
prompt: Tracking two-shot at walking pace: [SUBJECT A] apologizing in motion because stillness would be unbearable — eyes mostly down at the sidewalk seams, glancing sideways at [SUBJECT B] after each hard admission to check the damage. [SUBJECT B] walks eyes-forward, making them earn it, the forgiveness arriving first as a slowing of pace, then a shoulder bump, before any words confirm it. The camera eases its speed with theirs.

title: Whisper at the Party
prompt: Tight coverage in a loud room: [SUBJECT A] leans to [SUBJECT B]'s ear, [LOCATION: the party] smeared to warm bokeh and muffled bass around them — the whisper's content readable only in [SUBJECT B]'s face: eyes fixed on a stranger across the room as instructed, then widening one millimeter, then deliberately casual. A sip of a drink to cover the swallow. Both wearing party smiles over entirely different conversations now. The toast happening around them includes them not at all.

title: Kitchen Island Catch-Up
prompt: Medium coverage across a kitchen island: [SUBJECT A] cooking, [SUBJECT B] on a stool with wine, the conversation easy and overlapping — until a name surfaces and the chopping rhythm hitches, just once. [SUBJECT B] catches it, eyes moving from the knife to their friend's face, and gently sets the wine down: the universal sign for tell me. Steam, pendant warmth, the real talk happening while hands stay busy on purpose.

title: Waiting Room, 3 AM
prompt: Hospital waiting room two-shot, locked: [SUBJECT A] and [SUBJECT B] in plastic chairs under flat fluorescent, a vending machine humming color into the corner. Dialogue arrives in fragments with whole minutes between — eyes on the doors every time they swing, hope and dread doing the same face. One reaches over without looking and takes the other's hand; the conversation continues through the grip more than the words. The doors swing. Both stand.

title: The Reunion: Wide to Close
prompt: Reunion scene built in shrinking shot sizes: the wide — [SUBJECT A] spotting [SUBJECT B] across [LOCATION: a station concourse], both freezing in the crowd's current; the medium — the walk toward each other, eyes locked the whole way, chins fighting; the close — face to face, years inspected in seconds, a laugh and tears arriving in the same breath; the final tight two-shot of the hug, eyes shut hard over each other's shoulders. Coverage as approach.

title: Curbside Handoff
prompt: Custody handoff at the curb: the backpack passed, the schedule confirmed in the flat liturgy both have memorized — eyes meeting only over the practical items, never the past. The kid hugs one, then the other, the adults' faces doing parallel work above the embrace: love down, armor across. "Sunday at six." "Sunday at six." A beat where something almost gets said. The car doors close on it.

title: Old Friends, New Distance
prompt: Reunion drinks in [LOCATION: a quiet bar booth]: the first twenty minutes of laughter shot loose and warm — then the catch-up runs out of past and hits the present, and the rhythm stiffens. Eyes start landing on glasses instead of faces. "We should do this more," said by the one checking the time. The matched pushes find both faces realizing the same thing at different speeds: the friendship is now a museum they both love.

title: The Haggle
prompt: Playful negotiation across a stall in [LOCATION: an open-air market]: prices volleyed like a rally, both parties performing outrage they don't feel — the vendor's hand on heart, eyes delighted; [SUBJECT]'s theatrical walk-away, counting steps until the call-back comes (three; it's always three). The handshake at the inevitable number both knew at the start, grins finally unhidden, an extra fig thrown in for the show's quality.

title: Carpool Window
prompt: Hood-mounted two-shot, school run: the teenager in the passenger seat answers in vowels until the parent stops asking — and three blocks of silence later, eyes fixed out the window, the kid starts actually talking. The parent's masterclass: eyes on the road, voice neutral, hands betraying nothing, every instinct to turn and react strangled in service of keeping the channel open. The school arrives too soon for both of them.

title: Stakeout Hour Nine
prompt: Two detectives, hour nine, [LOCATION: a dark sedan]: the banter has burned through sports and exes and arrived at the real things people only say facing forward — eyes on the doorway across the street, the conversation's intimacy enabled by never looking at each other. A thermos passed without a glance. "You ever think about the one we didn't —" "Every day." The doorway opens; both go silent and still mid-sentence, transformed.

title: Meeting the Parents
prompt: The dinner-table gauntlet: [SUBJECT] fielding the father's friendly interrogation while the partner's knee presses reassurance under [LOCATION: the family dining table] — coverage catching the eye-traffic: mother and daughter trading micro-verdicts across the gravy, father's smile reaching his eyes exactly once, on the answer about work ethic. The joke that lands wrong; the joke that saves it. Dessert arriving as a ceasefire.

title: The Offer Refused
prompt: Mentor and protégé at dusk in [LOCATION: a corner office]: the offer slid across the desk is everything wanted five years ago. [SUBJECT] reads it fully — eyes moving line to line, buying time — then slides it back. "You taught me to know the price of things." The mentor's face does a strange double move: the jaw disappointed, the eyes proud. Neither names which one wins. The handshake longer than the meeting.

title: Hallway Decision
prompt: Two siblings in [LOCATION: a hospital hallway] with the form neither wants to hold: the argument conducted in furious whispers, eyes raw, the years of who-did-more detonating in shorthand only they can parse. A nurse passes; both go statue. The turn: "She told me something, last spring. She made me promise." Eyes meeting properly for the first time in the scene — and the fight leaves both bodies at once. The pen changes hands.

title: Packing Logistics
prompt: The quiet end: two people dividing [LOCATION: a shared bedroom] into boxes with terrible politeness — "Do you want the lamp?" carrying everything the big speeches would have. Eyes meet over the photo neither claims. The tape gun's shriek punctuating sentences neither finishes. One small laugh at a found object, instantly grieved, because laughing still works and that's the unbearable part. The last box labeled in both their handwriting.

title: Night Train Strangers
prompt: Sleeper-car honesty between strangers on [LOCATION: a night train]: the conversation only anonymity permits — real things traded across the dim cabin, faces lit by stations sweeping past, eyes meeting in the window's reflection more than directly. "I've never told anyone that." "You still haven't, really." A shared smile at the loophole. At their stop, names almost exchanged — then, by mutual eye-agreement, deliberately not.

title: Master Interview Setup
prompt: Classic documentary interview master: [SUBJECT] seated slightly off-center against a softly lit study, bookshelf bokeh, a single warm key with gentle wrap and a hair light separating them from the depth. They listen to an unheard question, take a breath, and begin — then stop, look down, and start again from somewhere truer. 50mm at eye level, the framing patient. The second take is the film.

title: Rostrum Photo Drift
prompt: Rostrum camera on a single creased black-and-white photograph: a slow drift across a 1950s street crowd, the move settling on one face at the edge of frame — a young woman caught mid-laugh, unaware of the camera — then a gentle push closer until the grain itself becomes the image. Classic rostrum pan-and-scan archival grammar at its most patient. A letter being read in voiceover, paper texture in the silence between lines.

title: Process: Bread at 4 AM
prompt: Observational vérité in a bakery at 4 AM: flour dust hanging in fluorescent light, [BAKER]'s hands folding dough with thirty years of economy, the camera simply present — racks rolling, oven doors breathing heat into the lens, the radio murmuring. No interviews, no music; the process is the narrative, rising and proofing in real rhythm. Shot at working distance, never in the way. The first loaf cracked open, steaming.

title: Street Portrait Series
prompt: A series of locked street portraits: strangers asked to simply stand and be looked at for ten seconds — a crossing guard, a teen with a skateboard, a woman with grocery bags — each framed identically in open shade at 85mm, [LOCATION: the city] soft behind them. The first seconds are performance; then the face settles into itself, and that's the frame that matters. City ambience continuous beneath, binding them into one place.

title: 1968 Protest Reel
prompt: Authentic 1968 news-film aesthetic: handheld 16mm black-and-white of a street protest — banners surging, the cameraman jostled, focus hunting in the crush, a police line resolving through tear-gas haze. Sprocket judder, hair in the gate, the exposure blowing out when the crowd opens to sky. A reporter's voice shouting into a wind-battered mic. History shot by someone who didn't know how it would end.

title: Night Shift Portrait
prompt: Portrait of a night-shift worker: [SUBJECT] alone in a depot at 3 AM, the documentary camera keeping respectful distance — them eating from a lunchbox on a forklift's step, sodium light through high windows, their voice in interview overlay: "Everybody's asleep when I'm useful. You get used to the quiet. Mostly." Long observational holds, steam from a thermos cup. Dignity in the unglamorous hour.

title: Hands of the Last Craftsman
prompt: Macro study of a craftsman's hands — the last in his region who makes [CRAFT] the old way: scarred knuckles, a lifetime's calluses, the tool moving with no wasted motion, shavings curling in north window light. The lens widens once: a small workshop, one bulb, a wall of tools each shaped by decades of one grip. His voice, unhurried: "My father said the hands learn last, but they forget never."

title: City Symphony Dawn
prompt: City-symphony montage from pre-dawn to rush hour: street sweepers' brushes in close-up, shutters rolling up in sequence down a market street, a bridge's lights dying as the sky pinks, bakers and fishmongers and transit drivers beginning their choreographies — the cuts accelerating with the light until the streets fill and the individual dissolves into the flow. 1920s symphony-film tradition, scored by the city's own found sound.

title: 8mm Family Reel
prompt: Home-movie texture: silent 8mm footage of a 1970s backyard birthday — overexposed sky, kids ghosting trails as they run, a grandmother waving the camera away then laughing, frame-skip on the cake's candles, the reel's edge flaring orange before the splice jumps to a beach from a different summer. Projector clatter on the soundtrack, and a present-day voice saying "...there — stop. That's him."

title: Contact Sheet Testimony
prompt: A photographer's contact sheet on a lightbox: the rostrum camera drifting across thirty-six frames of the same minute of history — the crowd, the smoke, the turn — while a grease pencil circles one frame in red as the photographer's voice explains why this one, what happened a half-second later, and which frames they've never printed. The unchosen images holding the heavier story.

title: Last Day of the Plant
prompt: Vérité on a factory's final shift: machines being powered down for the last time and wiped clean anyway, a worker chalking a farewell on a press that outlived three owners, lockers emptied into milk crates, the floor's lifetime of noise replaced by individual footsteps. At the gate, the shift walks out together and pauses, unplanned, for one look back. The camera stays at respectful distance throughout.

title: Hands and the Telling
prompt: Oral-history setup: [SUBJECT], ninety years old, in window light telling it — the camera locked on their face for the long passages, cutting only to their hands, which retell everything in parallel: smoothing the tablecloth at the hard part, measuring a remembered distance, going still entirely at the name they haven't said in decades. The interviewer never heard. The pauses kept whole.

title: The Morning After the March
prompt: Aftermath vérité at dawn: the avenue where two hundred thousand stood, now empty — placards drifted against curbs like leaves, a lost shoe upright on a barrier, city crews feeding signs into a compactor while one volunteer peels tape from a statue's base. A tracking shot at walking pace reading the ground's archaeology. What remains of a voice after the voice goes home.

title: Set at Magic Hour
prompt: Behind-the-scenes vérité on a film set racing magic hour: the AD's countdown rippling through departments, a condor light blooming against the real dusk it imitates, dolly track laid by headlamp, the lead actor rolling their neck alone behind a flat while forty people make a hallway exist. "Rolling" silencing the field like a spell. One take inside the light, and the cheer when it's caught.

title: Through the Translator
prompt: Interview in two languages, shot as a triangle: the subject answers at length, eyes alive with the telling — the cut to the interviewer, understanding nothing, reading everything from face alone — then the translator's compression, and the subject watching their own story shrink into another tongue, nodding at the parts that survive. The most important answer is the one the subject gives directly to the lens, untranslated, and the film leaves it that way.

title: Last Stock-Take
prompt: A closing shop's final inventory as still-life cinema: the camera patient on [LOCATION: a hardware store]'s last shelves — bins of singular screws, a wall of keys for doors that moved away, the hand-labeled drawers in a script that took fifty years to get this confident. The owner counts aloud off-screen, voice ordinary until one item makes it stop. Insert: a child's growth chart penciled inside the stockroom door. The count resumes, thicker.

title: Frame by Frame
prompt: Verification vérité: investigators around a monitor in [LOCATION: a dim edit bay], a phone video advanced frame by frame — the cursor circling a reflection in a shop window, a shadow's angle argued over, sun position checked against a mapping tool. The room's eyes narrow in unison at frame 412. "There. That's the same van." The video plays at speed one final time, transformed by what they now can't unsee.

title: Four Buses
prompt: One worker's commute, observed whole: alarm in the dark, the first bus at 4:50 — window-sleep mastered, head bumping glass — the transfer sprint, the second bus's regulars nodded to by name, sunrise purchased through a third window, the final stretch walked. [SUBJECT]'s voiceover doing the math of hours and wages without complaint, eyes out the window doing something less tidy. The shift door at 7:58. The film ends where the workday starts.

title: Tuesday Rehearsal
prompt: Community-choir vérité in [LOCATION: a church hall]: folding chairs, cough drops shared down the rows, the director's hands sculpting air — a hymn falling apart twice at the same bar to communal laughter, then the third pass where it suddenly assembles and every face changes at once, eyes lifting off the sheet music mid-phrase. The shot drifts down the rows catching that lift face by face. After: stacked chairs, and one soprano humming it into the parking lot.

title: The Keeper's Log
prompt: A lighthouse keeper's logbook read across decades: rostrum macro on entries in fading ink — "wind NNE, lamp trimmed, whales south" — intercut with the location now: the same sea through the same glass, automated lamp turning unattended. The last keeper's final entry given the screen alone, in silence, then his recorded voice, old and amused: "I wrote the weather for forty years. Nobody ever wrote back."

title: Verdict Steps
prompt: The courthouse-steps scrum, inside it: handheld jostled among lenses and boom mics as the doors open — the family emerging first, faces doing opposite weathers, the lawyer's statement read over forty shutter-bursts a second. The camera finds the quieter story at the scrum's edge: the juror who stayed, watching from the bottom step, eyes following the family with something they'll carry home unnamed.

title: A Year in the Hives
prompt: Beekeeping observed across a year: macro of winter cluster's slow-motion shiver, the spring inspection's lifted frame heavy with new comb, [SUBJECT]'s bare-handed calm narrating queens by temperament like difficult colleagues — the swarm-day scramble, ladder against [LOCATION: a neighbor's plum tree], the soft pour of thousands into a cardboard box. The harvest's amber pull. Stings shown, shrugged. "They know me. Mostly."

title: Counter Chorus
prompt: One locked frame on [LOCATION: a diner counter] across a whole morning, jump-cut: the 5 AM solitaries with their papers; the 7 AM trades crowd's overlapping orders; the 9 AM retirees solving the world over refills — the waitress the constant through every cut, coffee pot a metronome, her banter calibrated per customer. Faces change; the stools' politics persist. By the closing cut she's eating her own breakfast, finally, on the customer side.

title: Public Comment
prompt: Town-hall vérité in [LOCATION: a municipal chamber]: the zoning item nobody expected to fill the room — the two-camera coverage trading between the podium's three-minute speeches (the trembling note cards, the practiced ones, the off-script grief) and the council's faces doing fatigue, calculus, and twice, genuine surprise. The vote called by show of hands; the room's collective inhale counted in the same frame as the tally.

title: Metropolis Dawn Push
prompt: Aerial establishing shot pushing slowly toward a metropolis skyline at first light — towers emerging from low haze, the grid of streets still glowing sodium-orange below while the highest glass catches new pink sun. A river cuts through the frame, one early ferry drawing a wake. Unhurried push, the city presented like a chessboard before the game. Distant rumble, gulls, a single siren threading the quiet.

title: Main Street Wakes
prompt: Ground-level establishing shot tracking slowly down a small-town main street at opening time: striped awnings cranking out, a shopkeeper flipping a CLOSED sign, sprinklers ticking over a strip of grass by the war memorial, one pickup idling outside the cafe. Flags barely moving. Morning gold raking the brick storefronts. The whole town's character delivered in thirty unhurried feet of sidewalk.

title: Storm Manor
prompt: Establishing shot of an isolated gothic manor on a hill against a churning storm sky — bare trees thrashing along the drive, a single lit window in the east wing, iron gates swinging unlatched. A slow low push up the driveway as lightning silhouettes the rooflines and gargoyles. Rain starting in fat drops on the lens. The house introduced as a character with intentions.

title: Diner at 2 AM
prompt: Static establishing shot across an empty highway: a roadside diner at 2 AM, its neon sign buzzing OPEN in red cursive, interior fluorescence spilling onto three parked cars and a puddle holding the whole scene upside down. Through the glass, tiny figures — a waitress pouring coffee, one customer at the counter. Moths circling the sign. A lonely realist-painting composition, isolation rendered in light. Crickets and the neon's hum.

title: County Hospital Morning
prompt: Establishing shot of a county hospital under flat overcast: slow tilt down from the HOSPITAL sign and rooftop vents to the entrance below — an ambulance parked silent, a man in a gown wheeled out to smoke by the doors, a woman hurrying in with flowers wrapped in plastic. Automatic doors breathing open and shut. Muted palette, institutional brick. The building where every story in town eventually passes through.

title: First Bell
prompt: High wide establishing shot of a brick high school as the first bell rings: buses lined up disgorging streams of students, the crowd currents merging at the front steps, a teacher holding the door, one kid sprinting across the lawn with a backpack half-open and papers escaping. Flag snapping at the pole. Morning light, long shadows across the parking lot. An ecosystem arriving all at once. Single continuous take unless a lens switch is written; characters stay consistent and singular; grounded, stable physics throughout.
negative: deformed or morphing bodies, extra limbs, duplicated or merging characters, warped faces, stutter-stepping, sliding feet, clipping through objects, sudden environment or character changes, flicker, jitter, cloned or copy-pasted extras, crowd members merging, faces repeating

title: Precinct in Snow
prompt: Establishing shot of a city police precinct at winter dusk — snow falling through the glow of twin green entrance lamps, a cruiser parked half-buried at the curb, two detectives climbing the salted steps with coffee, their breath trailing. Wire-mesh windows lit warm against the blue cold. A slow push toward the doors as a radio call crackles from the cruiser. Procedural mood set in one frame.

title: The Lit Window
prompt: Night establishing shot of a quiet suburban street — identical dark houses, sprinkler mist drifting through a streetlamp cone — except one house with a single upstairs window lit. A slow push down the street toward that window, a silhouette crossing behind the curtain once. Cicadas, a dog barking two yards over, a car passing somewhere parallel. Suburbia asleep, except for whoever's story this is.

title: Desert Motel Vacancy
prompt: Static wide establishing shot of a 1960s desert motel at dusk: a googie-style VACANCY sign flickering against a magenta sky, a kidney pool glowing unattended, one dusty sedan parked outside room 7 where the curtain is drawn but the light is on. Power lines sagging toward distant mesas. Heat still rising off the lot. The kind of place where plots check in. Sign buzz and desert wind.

title: Tower of Glass
prompt: Establishing shot at the base of a corporate headquarters: a slow tilt up the tower's mirrored face — clouds sliding across forty floors of glass, the company logo enormous and backlit at the crown, tiny suited figures revolving through the doors below. The tilt's patience makes the building feel less constructed than grown. Cold blue grade, traffic muted to a hum. Power expressed as vertical distance.

title: Harbor Village Dawn
prompt: Aerial drift establishing a fishing village at dawn: painted boats nodding at their moorings, smoke rising straight from two chimneys, nets hung along the seawall, a lone figure walking the pier with a thermos. Pink light on wet cobblestones, gulls wheeling through frame. The drift slows over the harbor mouth where the fleet's first boat chugs out. A place introduced through its morning rituals.

title: Black SUV at the Gates
prompt: Low static establishing shot at the gates of a walled estate: a black SUV rolls into frame and stops, engine idling, tinted windows giving nothing back. A long pause — then the iron gates shudder and begin to open inward, revealing a poplar-lined drive to a distant mansion. The SUV eases through, gravel crackling, and the gates seal the frame again. Gray light, no music. Arrival as threat assessment.

title: Taxi to the Brownstone
prompt: Establishing shot on a wet brownstone street at evening: a yellow taxi pulls to the curb in frame, brake lights blooming red in the wet asphalt. [SUBJECT] steps out with one suitcase, pays through the window, and stands a long beat looking up at the building as the cab pulls away — the camera panning gently to hold both them and the lit stoop. Rain just ended, gutters running. A homecoming or a reckoning.

title: Pickup at the Ranch
prompt: Wide static establishing shot of a ranch at late afternoon: a dusty pickup turns off the county road in the far distance, trailing a slow plume up the long dirt drive — past cattle guards and a leaning mailbox — finally rolling to a stop by the porch in the foreground. [SUBJECT] climbs out, stretches a long drive out of their back, and a dog rounds the barn at a sprint to meet them. Hard amber light, insects, screen door.

title: Helipad Arrival
prompt: Crane-height establishing shot on a skyscraper helipad: a helicopter flares and settles dead center, rotor wash flattening the wind cones, the city grid hazy far below. [SUBJECT] ducks out in a tailored coat, one hand holding it closed against the downdraft, flanked by two aides with cases, striding for the rooftop door without a glance at the view. The chopper lifts the second they clear. Power travels by air.

title: Last Bus Into Town
prompt: Static wide establishing shot of a one-bench bus stop at the edge of a small town at blue hour: the intercity bus hisses into frame, door folding open to spill warm interior light onto the gravel. [SUBJECT] steps down with a duffel bag — the only passenger — and the bus sighs shut and pulls away, taillights shrinking down the empty highway. They stand in the sudden quiet, town lights beginning behind them.

title: Mega-Crossing at Night
